text:
This chapter draws on the perspectives of administration, finance, and politics to describe the historical development of Japanese central–local relations. Using the bifurcation model of “interfusion” and “separation,” it structurally identifies the governing characteristics of Japan. At the time of Japan’s modernization in the Meiji period, the Japanese imitated the systems of contintental Europe and chose to introduce an interfusion system in which policy decisions are implemented by the local government agency rather than by an agent of the national government. The interfusion system has remained robust, despite occasional shifts in the direction of central–local relations, whether a functional centralization by the wartime regime, the structural decentralization enforced by the occupying powers during the post-war period, or the additional wave of reforms of the 1990s and beyond.

The interfusion model has been confirmed through large-scale municipal mergers and the creation of a strong system of fiscal constraint. Japan has one of the highest ratios of subnational public expenditure to total public sector expenditure in the world, but this is justified as being possible only in a highly integrated central-local system. The interfused structure of the Japanese state, which has provided a basis for the country’s politico-administrative system, has also survived the post-2000 challenges associated with the country’s declining population and its increasing ability to cope with large-scale crises.
